main_paper.raw: {"methods":["Partial Vector Freezing (PVF)","λ-SecAgg (integration of SAP with PVF)","Disrupting Variables Element for PVF"],"problems":["High computation cost of Secure Aggregation Protocols (SAPs) in federated learning due to per-entry processing of large update vectors","Communication overhead in secure aggregation for large models","Potential privacy leakage in aggregation protocols","Trade-off between efficiency and privacy in secure aggregation"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Computation time","Communication overhead/reduction","Speedup factor"],"results":[{"metric":"Speedup (λ=100)","value":"up to 99.5×"},{"metric":"Communication reduction (λ=100)","value":"up to 32.3×"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes Partial Vector Freezing (PVF), a portable module that reduces computation cost in SAPs without additional communication overhead or loss of aggregation accuracy.","Introduces λ-SecAgg, which integrates SAP with PVF to freeze most of the vector and only process a fraction (1/λ) in SAP, with the ability to recover the full aggregate.","Develops the Disrupting Variables Element to prevent privacy leakage from PVF.","Demonstrates that PVF can be seamlessly integrated with various SAPs and maintains privacy under semi-honest and active adversary settings.","Provides comprehensive experimental evaluation with 7 baselines and 5 types of masking schemes, showing significant speedup and communication reduction."]}

methods: Partial Vector Freezing (PVF)
λ-SecAgg (integration of SAP with PVF)
Disrupting Variables Element for PVF
problems: High computation cost of Secure Aggregation Protocols (SAPs) in federated learning due to per-entry processing of large update vectors
Communication overhead in secure aggregation for large models
Potential privacy leakage in aggregation protocols
Trade-off between efficiency and privacy in secure aggregation
novelty_claims: Proposes Partial Vector Freezing (PVF), a portable module that reduces computation cost in SAPs without additional communication overhead or loss of aggregation accuracy.
Introduces λ-SecAgg, which integrates SAP with PVF to freeze most of the vector and only process a fraction (1/λ) in SAP, with the ability to recover the full aggregate.
Develops the Disrupting Variables Element to prevent privacy leakage from PVF.
Demonstrates that PVF can be seamlessly integrated with various SAPs and maintains privacy under semi-honest and active adversary settings.
Provides comprehensive experimental evaluation with 7 baselines and 5 types of masking schemes, showing significant speedup and communication reduction.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Partial Vector Freezing (PVF): a module that freezes a portion of the private vector via linear transformations, reducing the number of entries processed in Secure Aggregation Protocols (SAP) to 1/λ of the original size","User Commitment Extension: ensures uniqueness of users’ original vectors to prevent malicious computations","Result Verification Extension: ensures correctness of linear transformations to prevent malicious server-side computations","δ-security Extension: enhances privacy when adversaries have prior knowledge of part of the private vector"],"problems":["High computation cost of Secure Aggregation Protocols (SAP) in Federated Learning due to processing every entry in user vectors","Trade-off between privacy and utility in Differential Privacy-based SAP","Lack of methods to reduce the number of entries processed in SAP while ensuring intact aggregation of all vector entries","Inefficiency and impracticality of Homomorphic Encryption-based SAP due to high computation and communication costs"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Computation speedup (acceleration factor)","Communication reduction (compression factor)"],"results":[{"metric":"Computation speedup (λ=100)","value":"up to 99.5×"},{"metric":"Communication reduction (λ=100, HE-based SAP)","value":"up to 32.3×"},{"metric":"Computation speedup (as λ increases)","value":"approaches nearly 1000×"}],"novelty_claims":["First work to reduce the number of entries processed in SAP while ensuring aggregation of all entries in the vector","PVF is a portable, decoupled module that can be integrated with various SAP without additional communication overhead or loss of accuracy/security","PVF enables significant computation and communication cost reduction, making previously impractical SAP (e.g., HE-based) viable for real-world applications","Comprehensive evaluation across 8 baselines and 6 SAP categories, providing a broad comparison not seen in prior work","PVF supports extensions for input consistency, result verification, and enhanced privacy against adversaries with partial knowledge"]}

methods: Partial Vector Freezing (PVF): a module that freezes a portion of the private vector via linear transformations, reducing the number of entries processed in Secure Aggregation Protocols (SAP) to 1/λ of the original size
User Commitment Extension: ensures uniqueness of users’ original vectors to prevent malicious computations
Result Verification Extension: ensures correctness of linear transformations to prevent malicious server-side computations
δ-security Extension: enhances privacy when adversaries have prior knowledge of part of the private vector
problems: High computation cost of Secure Aggregation Protocols (SAP) in Federated Learning due to processing every entry in user vectors
Trade-off between privacy and utility in Differential Privacy-based SAP
Lack of methods to reduce the number of entries processed in SAP while ensuring intact aggregation of all vector entries
Inefficiency and impracticality of Homomorphic Encryption-based SAP due to high computation and communication costs
novelty_claims: First work to reduce the number of entries processed in SAP while ensuring aggregation of all entries in the vector
PVF is a portable, decoupled module that can be integrated with various SAP without additional communication overhead or loss of accuracy/security
PVF enables significant computation and communication cost reduction, making previously impractical SAP (e.g., HE-based) viable for real-world applications
Comprehensive evaluation across 8 baselines and 6 SAP categories, providing a broad comparison not seen in prior work
PVF supports extensions for input consistency, result verification, and enhanced privacy against adversaries with partial knowledge

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LightSecAgg protocol: a secure aggregation protocol for federated learning based on one-shot aggregate-mask reconstruction via mask encoding/decoding, rather than pairwise random-seed reconstruction","Modular federated training system design with optimized on-device parallelization, enabling computational overlap between model training and mask encoding, and concurrent receiving/sending of chunked masks","Support for both synchronous and asynchronous federated learning settings"],"problems":["High computational and communication overhead in secure aggregation protocols for federated learning, especially with user dropouts","Scalability limitations of existing secure aggregation protocols as the number of users and dropouts increases","Lack of secure aggregation protocols compatible with asynchronous federated learning","Need for efficient, privacy-preserving, and dropout-resilient model aggregation in federated learning"],"datasets":["FEMNIST","CIFAR-10","Google Landmarks Dataset 23k"],"metrics":["Total training time","Speedup over baseline protocols (SecAgg, SecAgg+)","Scalability with number of users","Bandwidth usage"],"results":[{"metric":"Speedup over SecAgg","value":"8.5× to 12.7×"},{"metric":"Speedup over SecAgg+","value":"2.9× to 4.4×"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LightSecAgg, a secure aggregation protocol that reconstructs a single aggregate-mask for surviving users, independent of the number of dropped users, reducing server-side complexity and runtime","First secure aggregation protocol compatible with asynchronous federated learning without relying on differential privacy or trusted execution environments","Provides a modular, open-sourced FL system with secure aggregation built on modern deep learning frameworks (e.g., PyTorch, ResNet) with system and security co-design","Demonstrates significant empirical speedup and scalability improvements over state-of-the-art protocols in realistic FL settings with large numbers of users and high-resolution datasets","Does not require a trusted third party, unlike some prior aggregate-mask approaches, and requires less randomness generation and storage at each user"]}

methods: LightSecAgg protocol: a secure aggregation protocol for federated learning based on one-shot aggregate-mask reconstruction via mask encoding/decoding, rather than pairwise random-seed reconstruction

Modular federated training system design with optimized on-device parallelization, enabling computational overlap between model training and mask encoding, and concurrent receiving/sending of chunked masks
Support for both synchronous and asynchronous federated learning settings
problems: High computational and communication overhead in secure aggregation protocols for federated learning, especially with user dropouts
Scalability limitations of existing secure aggregation protocols as the number of users and dropouts increases
Lack of secure aggregation protocols compatible with asynchronous federated learning
Need for efficient, privacy-preserving, and dropout-resilient model aggregation in federated learning
novelty_claims: Proposes LightSecAgg, a secure aggregation protocol that reconstructs a single aggregate-mask for surviving users, independent of the number of dropped users, reducing server-side complexity and runtime
First secure aggregation protocol compatible with asynchronous federated learning without relying on differential privacy or trusted execution environments
Provides a modular, open-sourced FL system with secure aggregation built on modern deep learning frameworks (e.g., PyTorch, ResNet) with system and security co-design
Demonstrates significant empirical speedup and scalability improvements over state-of-the-art protocols in realistic FL settings with large numbers of users and high-resolution datasets
Does not require a trusted third party, unlike some prior aggregate-mask approaches, and requires less randomness generation and storage at each user

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SparseSecAgg: a secure aggregation protocol with gradient sparsification","Pairwise sparsification: sparsification pattern determined by pairwise multiplicative random masks","Pairwise additive masking for privacy in aggregation"],"problems":["High communication overhead in secure aggregation for federated learning","Inapplicability of conventional gradient sparsification to secure aggregation due to privacy leakage","Trade-off between privacy and communication efficiency in privacy-preserving federated learning"],"datasets":["CIFAR-10","MNIST"],"metrics":["Communication overhead","Wall clock training time","Test accuracy","Convergence rate","Privacy guarantee (parameter T)"],"results":[{"metric":"Communication overhead reduction (CIFAR-10)","value":"7.8x"},{"metric":"Communication overhead reduction (MNIST)","value":"17.9x"},{"metric":"Wall clock training time speedup","value":"1.13x"}],"novelty_claims":["First secure aggregation protocol (SparseSecAgg) that supports gradient sparsification in federated learning","Novel pairwise sparsification process using pairwise multiplicative random masks to determine sparsification pattern","Quantification and theoretical analysis of the privacy-communication trade-off in secure aggregation with sparsification","Demonstration of significant reduction in communication overhead while maintaining privacy and convergence guarantees"]}

Pairwise sparsification: sparsification pattern determined by pairwise multiplicative random masks
Pairwise additive masking for privacy in aggregation
problems: High communication overhead in secure aggregation for federated learning

novelty_claims: First secure aggregation protocol (SparseSecAgg) that supports gradient sparsification in federated learning

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FastSecAgg: a scalable secure aggregation protocol for federated learning","FastShare: a novel multi-secret sharing scheme based on the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)"],"problems":["High computation and communication costs in secure aggregation for federated learning, especially when model size exceeds number of clients","Robustness to client dropouts in federated learning secure aggregation","Providing strong privacy guarantees against adaptive adversaries in federated learning","Scalability of secure aggregation protocols to large numbers of clients and high-dimensional model updates"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Computation cost (asymptotic complexity)","Communication cost (asymptotic complexity)","Adversary model (adaptive vs non-adaptive)","Dropout tolerance (worst-case vs average-case)"],"results":[{"metric":"Computation cost (server and client)","value":"O(L log N)"},{"metric":"Communication cost (server)","value":"O(LN + N^2)"},{"metric":"Communication cost (client)","value":"O(L + N)"},{"metric":"Adversary model","value":"Adaptive (secure against adaptive adversaries)"},{"metric":"Dropout tolerance","value":"Average-case (tolerates random dropouts of ~10% of clients)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes FastShare, a novel multi-secret sharing scheme based on FFT, achieving information-theoretic security and efficient recovery with O(N log N) complexity.","FastSecAgg achieves significantly lower computation cost (O(L log N)) than prior secure aggregation protocols while maintaining similar communication cost.","FastSecAgg is robust to random client dropouts (average-case) and secure against adaptive adversaries, unlike some prior works which only handle non-adaptive adversaries.","FastShare enables a trade-off between the number of secrets, privacy threshold, and dropout tolerance, tailored for federated learning scenarios."]}

methods: FastSecAgg: a scalable secure aggregation protocol for federated learning
FastShare: a novel multi-secret sharing scheme based on the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)
problems: High computation and communication costs in secure aggregation for federated learning, especially when model size exceeds number of clients
Robustness to client dropouts in federated learning secure aggregation
Providing strong privacy guarantees against adaptive adversaries in federated learning
Scalability of secure aggregation protocols to large numbers of clients and high-dimensional model updates

results.value: Average-case (tolerates random dropouts of ~10% of clients)

novelty_claims: Proposes FastShare, a novel multi-secret sharing scheme based on FFT, achieving information-theoretic security and efficient recovery with O(N log N) complexity.
FastSecAgg achieves significantly lower computation cost (O(L log N)) than prior secure aggregation protocols while maintaining similar communication cost.
FastSecAgg is robust to random client dropouts (average-case) and secure against adaptive adversaries, unlike some prior works which only handle non-adaptive adversaries.
FastShare enables a trade-off between the number of secrets, privacy threshold, and dropout tolerance, tailored for federated learning scenarios.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Turbo-Aggregate: a secure aggregation framework for federated learning","Multi-group circular strategy for model aggregation","Additive secret sharing for privacy and security","Aggregation redundancy via Lagrange coding for robustness against user dropouts","Support for both centralized and decentralized communication architectures"],"problems":["Quadratic overhead of secure model aggregation in federated learning as the number of users increases","Scalability of secure aggregation protocols to large numbers of users","Robustness to user dropouts and unavailability in federated learning","Ensuring privacy of individual user updates in the presence of dropouts or delays"],"datasets":["Amazon EC2 cloud (for distributed implementation and evaluation)"],"metrics":["Total running time","Speedup over state-of-the-art protocols","Aggregation overhead (complexity)","Robustness to user dropout rate"],"results":[{"metric":"Total running time growth","value":"Almost linear in the number of users"},{"metric":"Speedup over state-of-the-art","value":"Up to 40x with N=200 users"},{"metric":"Aggregation overhead","value":"O(N log N) vs O(N^2) for state-of-the-art"},{"metric":"Robustness to user dropout rate","value":"Up to 50% user dropout tolerated"},{"metric":"Performance under bandwidth constraints","value":"Substantial gain maintained even with severe bandwidth constraints"}],"novelty_claims":["First secure aggregation framework for federated learning with O(N log N) overhead, breaking the previous O(N^2) barrier","Provable robustness to up to 50% user dropout rate while maintaining privacy guarantees","Use of multi-group circular aggregation, additive secret sharing, and Lagrange coding for efficient, robust, and private aggregation","Experimental demonstration of up to 40x speedup over state-of-the-art protocols with 200 users","Maintains privacy and efficiency even in the presence of user dropouts and bandwidth constraints"]}

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["CoCoFL: Communication- and Computation-Aware Federated Learning via partial neural network (NN) freezing and quantization","Partial freezing of selected NN layers on constrained devices","Quantization of frozen layers to reduce computation and communication","Heuristic for device-local selection of layers to freeze/train based on resource profiling"],"problems":["Inefficiency and unfairness in federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous device resources","Low accuracy and poor contribution from constrained devices in FL, especially with non-iid data","High communication, computation, and memory requirements for FL on resource-constrained devices"],"datasets":["CIFAR10","XChest","LEAF benchmark data"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Fairness (accuracy parity)"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy (CoCoFL vs. state-of-the-art on CIFAR10, XChest, LEAF)","value":"CoCoFL achieves significantly higher accuracy than state-of-the-art subset-based techniques in both iid and non-iid settings"},{"metric":"Fairness (accuracy parity)","value":"CoCoFL increases fairness among participants compared to state-of-the-art methods"}],"novelty_claims":["First to combine partial freezing and quantization of NN layers in FL to adapt to device resource constraints while maintaining full model structure on all devices","Introduces a heuristic for server-independent, device-local selection of frozen/trained layers based on resource profiling","Demonstrates that subset-based techniques can be outperformed by simply excluding constrained devices, and that CoCoFL enables higher accuracy and fairness than both baselines and state-of-the-art methods","Shows that quantizing frozen layers while keeping trained layers at full precision enables large reductions in resource requirements without sacrificing accuracy"]}

methods: CoCoFL: Communication- and Computation-Aware Federated Learning via partial neural network (NN) freezing and quantization
Partial freezing of selected NN layers on constrained devices
Quantization of frozen layers to reduce computation and communication
Heuristic for device-local selection of layers to freeze/train based on resource profiling
problems: Inefficiency and unfairness in federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous device resources
Low accuracy and poor contribution from constrained devices in FL, especially with non-iid data

results.metric: Accuracy (CoCoFL vs. state-of-the-art on CIFAR10, XChest, LEAF)
results.value: CoCoFL achieves significantly higher accuracy than state-of-the-art subset-based techniques in both iid and non-iid settings
CoCoFL increases fairness among participants compared to state-of-the-art methods

novelty_claims: First to combine partial freezing and quantization of NN layers in FL to adapt to device resource constraints while maintaining full model structure on all devices
Introduces a heuristic for server-independent, device-local selection of frozen/trained layers based on resource profiling

Demonstrates that subset-based techniques can be outperformed by simply excluding constrained devices, and that CoCoFL enables higher accuracy and fairness than both baselines and state-of-the-art methods
Shows that quantizing frozen layers while keeping trained layers at full precision enables large reductions in resource requirements without sacrificing accuracy

Achieves logarithmic user-side computation complexity and linear server-side computation, improving over previous quadratic or linearithmic approaches.
Significantly reduces communication cost and computational burden compared to Bonawitz et al., Bell et al., and Ma et al.

methods: Efficient and dropout-resilient aggregation scheme for privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) leveraging homomorphic pseudorandom generator (HPRG) and Shamir secret sharing
Replacement of Diffie-Hellman key exchange for seed agreement with HPRG-based scheme
Integration with building blocks of SecAgg-based schemes
problems: Efficient aggregation in privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) with resilience to participant dropout
Reducing computation and communication overhead in secure aggregation protocols for federated learning
Ensuring privacy and security (against semi-honest and malicious adversaries) in large-scale PPML with resource-constrained and unreliable clients
datasets: CIFAR-10 (for image classification, as referenced in runtime comparison)
metrics: Dropout resilience (maximum number of tolerated dropouts)
Privacy guarantee (security against semi-honest and malicious adversaries)

results.metric: Runtime (CIFAR-10, similar neural network and server config)

results.value: secure against both semi-honest and malicious adversaries

novelty_claims: Proposes a scalable privacy-preserving aggregation scheme for PPML that is resilient to participant dropout at any time and secure against both semi-honest and active malicious adversaries.
Replaces communication-intensive Diffie-Hellman key exchange with a homomorphic pseudorandom generator (HPRG)-based scheme, significantly reducing communication overhead.
Integrates HPRG with Shamir secret sharing and SecAgg building blocks to achieve improved efficiency and stronger dropout-resilience.
Demonstrates that the runtime of the protocol decreases as the dropout rate increases, which is a novel property compared to prior schemes.
Achieves up to 6.37× runtime improvement over state-of-the-art schemes while maintaining strong privacy guarantees.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Federated Freeze A LoRA (FFA-LoRA): a modified LoRA approach for federated learning that freezes the non-zero initialized low-rank matrices and only updates the zero-initialized matrices, reducing communication and computation costs"],"problems":["Instability and performance degradation of LoRA in privacy-preserving federated learning due to data heterogeneity, multi-step local updates, amplified noise from differential privacy, and sensitivity to hyper-parameters","Discordance between local joint optimization and global aggregation of LoRA matrices in federated learning","Amplification of DP noise in LoRA's optimization process in federated learning","High communication and computational costs in federated fine-tuning of large language models"],"datasets":[],"metrics":[],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Identification and analysis of three key discordances when applying LoRA in privacy-preserving federated learning: mismatched local/global updates, amplified DP noise, and hyper-parameter sensitivity","Proposal of FFA-LoRA, which freezes the non-zero initialized matrices and only updates the zero-initialized matrices, halving the number of parameters to communicate and update compared to standard LoRA","FFA-LoRA provides more consistent performance and better computational efficiency than vanilla LoRA in privacy-preserving federated learning tasks","FFA-LoRA alleviates the identified discordances and is robust across different tasks, hyper-parameters, and privacy protection levels"]}

methods: Federated Freeze A LoRA (FFA-LoRA): a modified LoRA approach for federated learning that freezes the non-zero initialized low-rank matrices and only updates the zero-initialized matrices, reducing communication and computation costs
problems: Instability and performance degradation of LoRA in privacy-preserving federated learning due to data heterogeneity, multi-step local updates, amplified noise from differential privacy, and sensitivity to hyper-parameters
Discordance between local joint optimization and global aggregation of LoRA matrices in federated learning
Amplification of DP noise in LoRA's optimization process in federated learning
High communication and computational costs in federated fine-tuning of large language models
novelty_claims: Identification and analysis of three key discordances when applying LoRA in privacy-preserving federated learning: mismatched local/global updates, amplified DP noise, and hyper-parameter sensitivity

Proposal of FFA-LoRA, which freezes the non-zero initialized matrices and only updates the zero-initialized matrices, halving the number of parameters to communicate and update compared to standard LoRA
FFA-LoRA provides more consistent performance and better computational efficiency than vanilla LoRA in privacy-preserving federated learning tasks
FFA-LoRA alleviates the identified discordances and is robust across different tasks, hyper-parameters, and privacy protection levels

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FEDGEN (Federated Distillation via Generative Learning): a data-free knowledge distillation approach for federated learning that learns a lightweight generator from user model predictions and broadcasts it to users to guide local training"],"problems":["Heterogeneity in federated learning leading to slow convergence and poor generalization","Dependence of prior knowledge distillation methods on proxy datasets, which may not be available","Inefficient utilization of ensemble knowledge in guiding local model learning in FL"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Generalization performance","Number of communication rounds"],"results":[],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a data-free knowledge distillation approach for federated learning that does not require any external or proxy dataset","Introduces FEDGEN, which learns a lightweight generator from user model predictions to ensemble user information in a data-free manner","The generator is used to regulate local model training, providing an inductive bias and improving generalization under non-iid data","FEDGEN only requires the prediction layer of local models for knowledge extraction, making it suitable for scenarios with privacy or communication constraints","Demonstrates improved generalization performance and reduced communication rounds compared to state-of-the-art methods"]}

methods: FEDGEN (Federated Distillation via Generative Learning): a data-free knowledge distillation approach for federated learning that learns a lightweight generator from user model predictions and broadcasts it to users to guide local training
problems: Heterogeneity in federated learning leading to slow convergence and poor generalization
Dependence of prior knowledge distillation methods on proxy datasets, which may not be available
Inefficient utilization of ensemble knowledge in guiding local model learning in FL
novelty_claims: Proposes a data-free knowledge distillation approach for federated learning that does not require any external or proxy dataset
Introduces FEDGEN, which learns a lightweight generator from user model predictions to ensemble user information in a data-free manner
The generator is used to regulate local model training, providing an inductive bias and improving generalization under non-iid data
FEDGEN only requires the prediction layer of local models for knowledge extraction, making it suitable for scenarios with privacy or communication constraints
Demonstrates improved generalization performance and reduced communication rounds compared to state-of-the-art methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SASH: Secure Aggregation Scheme based on Seed Homomorphic Pseudo-Random Generator (SHPRG)"],"problems":["Efficient and secure aggregation in federated learning to prevent private training data leakage","Reducing computation and communication overhead in secure aggregation, especially with large numbers of clients and model parameters","Ensuring robustness to client dropouts in federated learning","Maintaining privacy against colluding adversaries in federated learning"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Computation complexity (client and server)","Communication cost","Dropout resilience","Privacy against colluding clients","Efficiency improvement (speedup over baseline)"],"results":[{"metric":"Efficiency improvement over baseline","value":"20x"},{"metric":"Computation complexity (client and server)","value":"O(M) (linear in model size, constant in number of clients)"},{"metric":"Dropout resilience","value":"Robust to up to N/3 arbitrary dropouts"},{"metric":"Privacy against colluding clients","value":"Secure against up to 2N/3 colluding clients"}],"novelty_claims":["First secure aggregation scheme for federated learning based on seed homomorphic pseudo-random generator (SHPRG)","Masking and demasking are simpler and more efficient than previous solutions, with linear computation complexity in model size and constant in number of clients","Significantly improves efficiency (up to 20x over baseline) especially in large-scale and high-dropout scenarios","Maintains robustness to client dropouts and strong privacy guarantees against colluding adversaries without extra overhead"]}

methods: SASH: Secure Aggregation Scheme based on Seed Homomorphic Pseudo-Random Generator (SHPRG)
problems: Efficient and secure aggregation in federated learning to prevent private training data leakage
Reducing computation and communication overhead in secure aggregation, especially with large numbers of clients and model parameters
Ensuring robustness to client dropouts in federated learning
Maintaining privacy against colluding adversaries in federated learning

results.value: O(M) (linear in model size, constant in number of clients)
Robust to up to N/3 arbitrary dropouts
Secure against up to 2N/3 colluding clients

novelty_claims: First secure aggregation scheme for federated learning based on seed homomorphic pseudo-random generator (SHPRG)
Masking and demasking are simpler and more efficient than previous solutions, with linear computation complexity in model size and constant in number of clients
Significantly improves efficiency (up to 20x over baseline) especially in large-scale and high-dropout scenarios
Maintains robustness to client dropouts and strong privacy guarantees against colluding adversaries without extra overhead

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Flamingo: a single-server secure aggregation protocol for multi-round federated learning","Lightweight dropout resilience protocol (small set of decryptors to remove masks)","Reusable secrets for masking inputs across multiple rounds","Per-round client neighborhood graph generation (clients locally choose pairwise masks)"],"problems":["Efficient and secure aggregation of client data in federated learning over multiple rounds","Reducing communication and setup overhead in secure aggregation protocols for federated learning","Ensuring dropout resilience (handling clients leaving mid-protocol) in secure aggregation","Maintaining model quality and privacy in federated learning with unstable and heterogeneous clients"],"datasets":["Extended MNIST","CIFAR-100"],"metrics":["End-to-end runtime (training time to convergence)","Model accuracy (convergence without loss in accuracy)","Number of clients included in aggregation"],"results":[{"metric":"End-to-end runtime (10-round pure summation task)","value":"Flamingo is 3x faster than BBGLR (including Flamingo’s one-time setup cost)"},{"metric":"Training time to convergence (Extended MNIST)","value":"Flamingo: ~40 minutes; BBGLR: ~3.5 hours (to reach same training accuracy)"},{"metric":"Model accuracy","value":"No loss in accuracy compared to non-private federated learning"}],"novelty_claims":["First single-server secure aggregation system supporting efficient multi-round aggregation for full federated learning sessions","One-time setup with reusable secrets across rounds, eliminating per-round setup overhead","Lightweight dropout resilience protocol allowing most clients to leave after submitting input, reducing bias and increasing client participation","Per-round client neighborhood graph generation performed locally by clients, reducing server involvement and communication","Significant reduction in end-to-end runtime and improved inclusion of clients compared to prior state-of-the-art protocols (e.g., BBGLR)"]}

methods: Flamingo: a single-server secure aggregation protocol for multi-round federated learning
Lightweight dropout resilience protocol (small set of decryptors to remove masks)
Reusable secrets for masking inputs across multiple rounds
Per-round client neighborhood graph generation (clients locally choose pairwise masks)
problems: Efficient and secure aggregation of client data in federated learning over multiple rounds
Reducing communication and setup overhead in secure aggregation protocols for federated learning
Ensuring dropout resilience (handling clients leaving mid-protocol) in secure aggregation
Maintaining model quality and privacy in federated learning with unstable and heterogeneous clients
metrics: Model accuracy (convergence without loss in accuracy)
Number of clients included in aggregation

results.value: Flamingo is 3x faster than BBGLR (including Flamingo’s one-time setup cost)
Flamingo: ~40 minutes; BBGLR: ~3.5 hours (to reach same training accuracy)
No loss in accuracy compared to non-private federated learning

novelty_claims: First single-server secure aggregation system supporting efficient multi-round aggregation for full federated learning sessions
One-time setup with reusable secrets across rounds, eliminating per-round setup overhead
Lightweight dropout resilience protocol allowing most clients to leave after submitting input, reducing bias and increasing client participation
Per-round client neighborhood graph generation performed locally by clients, reducing server involvement and communication
Significant reduction in end-to-end runtime and improved inclusion of clients compared to prior state-of-the-art protocols (e.g., BBGLR)
